text: The surgeon enters the retrorectus space using OptiView access. The left retrorectus space is developed with electrocautery, exposing the midline diastasis. The robot is used to incise the medial aspect of the left posterior rectus muscle fascia. The right-sided retrorectus fascia becomes visible, and the surgeon incises the right posterior rectus fascia until the muscle is exposed.
The surgeon identifies the hernia and makes an incision to dissect it. An intraperitoneal view confirms that no bowel is present inside the hernia, which is then completely excised.
The surgeon performs a right-side hemicomponent separation by incising the fascia to expose the transversus abdominis muscle, which is visible superiorly in the operative field.

The surgeon uses a barbed absorbable suture to close the peritoneal defect, which is now under less tension. The hernia defect is visible superiorly, and the closure is performed without tension. The final result of the peritoneal closure is shown. The surgeon then serially closes the hernia defect and the diastasis by placating the hernia sac with the same type of suture until the old defect is closed.

The surgical team measures the cavity, approximately thirty by thirty centimeters, for mesh placement. A synthetic permanent lightweight mesh is introduced laparoscopically through an eight-millimeter port and laid down in the cavity. Fibrin glue is applied to secure the mesh, and the pneumoperitoneum is released.
